# Build – Renewing a medical licence

**Generated by:** `bimstack:brief-to-prototypes` skill via `/build`
**Brief source:** `examples/discovery-renewing-medical-licence/01-problem-statement.md`
**Date:** 2026-05-16
**Status:** Three candidate prototypes, ready for user testing.

> These prototypes are throwaway alpha. They look like production but they aren't. The data is fake. Don't share them with citizens as a real service.

## The three hypotheses

Each prototype encodes a different design hypothesis about how a Bajan doctor could renew an annual licence. They're not three takes on the same idea – they make genuinely different bets that produce different signals in testing.

| # | Name | Hypothesis | Strongest for | Weakest for |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Phone-first individual renewal** | A doctor can renew end-to-end from a phone, on a slow connection, in about 10 minutes. Trident ID lookup, CPD upload, payment, certificate by email. | Private-practice doctors, doctors with stable smartphone access, doctors who already do most admin online. | Older doctors, doctors with poor connectivity, doctors with paper-only CPD. |
| 2 | **Hospital-mediated bulk renewal** | The hospital (QEH, polyclinic, private hospital) pre-fills almost everything from HR and CPD records, and the doctor's primary action is a single-tap confirmation. About 2 minutes. | Doctors employed by public institutions with mature HR. | Private-practice doctors, locums, doctors with employer-employee tensions. |
| 3 | **Hybrid assisted-digital** | Doctors choose at the start: renew online (~10 min) or book a 30-minute appointment at the Medical Council. Both paths first-class. | Doctors who'd rather speak to someone, doctors with paper records, doctors whose last renewal was difficult. | Time-poor doctors who'd rather not pick. |

The point of building three is **not to pick the winner now**. The point is to put real signal in front of the team so the alpha-to-beta decision is evidence-led, not opinion-led.

## Files

```
examples/build-renew-medical-licence/
├── README.md                              ← you are here
├── assumptions.md                         ← consolidated cross-prototype assumptions
├── test-plan.md                           ← the user-testing plan for all three
├── prototype-1-phone-first/index.html
├── prototype-2-hospital-bulk/index.html
└── prototype-3-hybrid-assisted/index.html
```

## How to open the prototypes

Open the `index.html` file in any browser. The flow is fully clickable. There is no server – everything is in one HTML file.

- Click through the journey end to end
- Walk it on a phone (open the file from a USB drive or a cloud share)
- Tap the gold "Show assumptions" button in the navy bimstack banner to see what the prototype is assuming

The assumptions panel is hidden by default so user testing sees a clean service. Toggle it for team review.

## How they look the same – on purpose

All three prototypes share:

- The same chrome (official banner, yellow GovBB header, alpha status, navy bimstack banner, navy footer)
- The same colour palette (navy, gold, off-white, charcoal)
- The Figtree font from Google Fonts
- The same fake citizen (Dr. Sarah K. Williams) so testers can compare like-for-like

Inside the chrome, the journeys differ. That's the test.

## How they look different – on purpose

- **Prototype 1** is a conventional question-per-page form flow. Most ceremony, longest time, most universal.
- **Prototype 2** opens with a navy hero strip ("From Queen Elizabeth Hospital") and a 24px "Confirm renewal" button at the climax. Shortest flow.
- **Prototype 3** opens with two equal-weight path cards. The choice is the design.

## Standards anchors

Every prototype carries an inline comment in `<head>` citing the standards it's anchored to. The shortlist:

- **Standard 1** (meet user needs) – every prototype starts from the discovery's user needs
- **Standard 3** (everyone can use it) – Prototype 3 specifically tests this against Prototypes 1 and 2
- **Standard 4** (simple language) – word-swap list applied across all three
- **Standard 5** (works first time) – Trident ID lookup, one-thing-per-page, confirmation page with reference + next-step + timing
- **Standard 7** (open, interoperable) – Trident ID, payment gateway, design system reused across all three
- **Standard 11** (trust, safety) – Prototype 2 stresses this; cyber engineer's notes in the assumptions

## Standards each prototype is hardest on

- **Prototype 1** is hardest on **Standard 3** (it depends on the doctor being able to renew on a phone alone)
- **Prototype 2** is hardest on **Standard 11** (it asks the citizen to trust the hospital's record as a regulatory primary)
- **Prototype 3** is hardest on **Standard 8** (it commits the Medical Council to staffed booked appointments)

User testing should focus on each prototype's weak standard.

## What to do next

1. **Read** the assumptions panel in each prototype, and the consolidated [assumptions.md](./assumptions.md)
2. **Test** with citizens following [test-plan.md](./test-plan.md) – 5–6 doctors per prototype, mixed cohorts
3. **Capture** what worked, what didn't, what surprised the team
4. **Feed back** with `/iterate prototype-X` – the prototype-iteration skill produces the next version with a changelog

Cycle time target: from build to first round of testing within **5 working days**. From feedback to next iteration within **3 working days**. Three full rounds in a month is achievable.

## What this build did not do

- It did not invent the user need. The brief comes from `examples/discovery-renewing-medical-licence/`.
- It did not pick a winner. That's the alpha gate (use `/assess`).
- It did not produce production code. These are throwaway prototypes.
- It did not consult the published `@govtech-bb/styles` CDN – it inlines an approximation. When MIST confirms the CDN URL, the prototypes can be switched to link to it instead.
- It did not threat-model the production service. `/threat-model` for that, in parallel.
